infobox.value: (**AAP**, lit. '[Common Man](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi "Aam Aadmi") Party') is a [political party in India](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_India "List of political parties in India"). It was founded on 26 November 2012 by [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal") and his then-companions,[[23]](#cite_note-23) following the [2011 Indian anti-corruption movement](/wiki/2011_Indian_anti-corruption_movement "2011 Indian anti-corruption movement") against then government of [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"). The AAP is currently the [governing party](/wiki/Ruling_party "Ruling party") in the [Indian state](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"). On 10 April 2023, the AAP was officially granted the status of [national party](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_India#National_parties "List of political parties in India") by the [ECI](/wiki/Election_Commission_of_India "Election Commission of India").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The party's election symbol is a [broom](/wiki/Broom "Broom").[[25]](#cite_note-25) APP was formerly a part of the [INDIA](/wiki/Indian_National_Developmental_Inclusive_Alliance "Indian National Developmental Inclusive Alliance") coalition until it's withdrawal in 2025.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-c281-27)

The **Aam Aadmi Party** (**AAP**, lit. '[Common Man](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi "Aam Aadmi") Party') is a [political party in India](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_India "List of political parties in India"). It was founded on 26 November 2012 by [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal") and his then-companions,[[23]](#cite_note-23) following the [2011 Indian anti-corruption movement](/wiki/2011_Indian_anti-corruption_movement "2011 Indian anti-corruption movement") against then government of [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"). The AAP is currently the [governing party](/wiki/Ruling_party "Ruling party") in the [Indian state](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"). On 10 April 2023, the AAP was officially granted the status of [national party](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_India#National_parties "List of political parties in India") by the [ECI](/wiki/Election_Commission_of_India "Election Commission of India").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The party's election symbol is a [broom](/wiki/Broom "Broom").[[25]](#cite_note-25) APP was formerly a part of the [INDIA](/wiki/Indian_National_Developmental_Inclusive_Alliance "Indian National Developmental Inclusive Alliance") coalition until it's withdrawal in 2025.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-c281-27)

The party came into existence following a difference of opinion between Kejriwal and activist [Anna Hazare](/wiki/Anna_Hazare "Anna Hazare") regarding the incorporation of electoral politics into the popular [2011 Indian anti-corruption movement](/wiki/2011_Indian_anti-corruption_movement "2011 Indian anti-corruption movement"), which had been demanding a [Jan Lokpal Bill](/wiki/Jan_Lokpal_Bill "Jan Lokpal Bill") since 2011.[[28]](#cite_note-The_Times_of_India-28) Hazare preferred the movement should remain politically unaligned, whereas Kejriwal felt the failure of the agitation route necessitated changes in the government's representation itself.[[28]](#cite_note-The_Times_of_India-28) On 3 December 2015, the Jan Lokpal Bill was passed by the AAP government with a majority in the Delhi legislative assembly.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Making its electoral debut in the [2013 Delhi Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2013_Delhi_Legislative_Assembly_election "2013 Delhi Legislative Assembly election"), the AAP emerged as the second-largest party and managed to form the government with support from Indian National Congress (INC) members of the assembly.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Kejriwal became the [Chief Minister of Delhi](/wiki/List_of_chief_ministers_of_Delhi "List of chief ministers of Delhi"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) but his government resigned 49 days after he could not pass the [Jan Lokpal Bill](/wiki/Jan_Lokpal_Bill "Jan Lokpal Bill") in the assembly, because of the lack of support from the INC.[[33]](#cite_note-33) After the [President's rule](/wiki/President%27s_rule "President's rule") in Delhi, in the [following 2015 elections](/wiki/2015_Delhi_Legislative_Assembly_election "2015 Delhi Legislative Assembly election"), the AAP won 67 of the 70 seats in the assembly and Kejriwal was again sworn in as the [Chief Minister of Delhi](/wiki/List_of_chief_ministers_of_Delhi "List of chief ministers of Delhi").[[34]](#cite_note-thehindu.com-34) In the subsequent [2020 Delhi Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2020_Delhi_Legislative_Assembly_election "2020 Delhi Legislative Assembly election"), AAP was re-elected as the governing party after winning 62 seats out of 70.[[35]](#cite_note-Delhi_2020_Hindu-35) The party suffered a sharp decline in the [2025 Delhi Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2025_Delhi_Legislative_Assembly_election "2025 Delhi Legislative Assembly election"), winning only 22 seats. It ultimately lost power to the [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party"), which obtained an absolute majority.Kejriwal and several other notable leaders lost their seats.

Outside Delhi, the AAP cemented its popularity when it emerged as the principal opposition party in the [2017 Punjab Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2017_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2017 Punjab Legislative Assembly election") after securing 20 seats. In the subsequent [2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2022_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election"), AAP was elected as the main governing party after winning 92 seats.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Following this, its member [Bhagwant Mann](/wiki/Bhagwant_Mann "Bhagwant Mann") was sworn in as the [Chief Minister of Punjab](/wiki/List_of_chief_ministers_of_Punjab_(India) "List of chief ministers of Punjab (India)").[[38]](#cite_note-38) In December 2022, the party emerged as the third front in the [politics of Gujarat](/wiki/Politics_of_Gujarat "Politics of Gujarat") after [2022 Gujarat Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2022_Gujarat_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Gujarat Legislative Assembly election"). It secured 12.92% of the votes polled and five seats in the Assembly.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Apart from Gujarat, AAP is also accorded the status of state party in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa").[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) In the [2024 Jammu and Kashmir Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2024_Jammu_and_Kashmir_Legislative_Assembly_election "2024 Jammu and Kashmir Legislative Assembly election"), AAP opened its account in [Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)") by winning of [Mehraj Malik](/wiki/Mehraj_Malik "Mehraj Malik") of AAP from [Doda Assembly constituency](/wiki/Doda_Assembly_constituency "Doda Assembly constituency") seat in Jammu region by a margin of 4538 votes, making him the first AAP MLA of [Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)").

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Aam_Aadmi_Party_logo.svg/250px-Aam_Aadmi_Party_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Aam_Aadmi_Party_logo.svg)

The party's former logo

### Formation

In the years immediately preceding the 2011 anti-corruption protests under the erstwhile [Second Manmohan Singh ministry led by UPA](/wiki/United_Progressive_Alliance "United Progressive Alliance") , there were examples of alleged corruption in the country; these included the [Adarsh Housing Society Scam](/wiki/Adarsh_Housing_Society_Scam "Adarsh Housing Society Scam"),[[42]](#cite_note-42) the [2010 housing loan scam](/wiki/2010_housing_loan_scam_in_India "2010 housing loan scam in India"),[[43]](#cite_note-43) the [Radia tapes controversy](/wiki/Radia_tapes_controversy "Radia tapes controversy"),[[44]](#cite_note-44) and the [2G spectrum case](/wiki/2G_spectrum_case "2G spectrum case").[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Massive anti-corruption protests and strikes](/wiki/2011_Indian_anti-corruption_movement "2011 Indian anti-corruption movement") were initiated by [Anna Hazare](/wiki/Anna_Hazare "Anna Hazare") in 2011 as a response to exposure of these financial scams and corruption. The aim was to press the government to enact a strong and effective Lokpal (Federal [Ombudsman](/wiki/Ombudsman "Ombudsman")) through a [Jan Lokpal Bill](/wiki/Jan_Lokpal_Bill "Jan Lokpal Bill").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma201439-46) Hazare was supported by a clutch of activists and professionals which became popularly known as [Team Anna](/wiki/Team_Anna "Team Anna"). Team Anna also included a civil-servant turned activist [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma201440–41-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Hazare had wanted to keep the movement politically neutral but Kejriwal considered that direct involvement in politics was necessary because attempts to obtain progress regarding the Jan Lokpal Bill through talks with existing political parties had, in his opinion, achieved nothing. A survey conducted on a Facebook page that purported to be operated by India Against Corruption[[49]](#cite_note-49) and other social networking services had indicated that there was wide support for politicisation.[[50]](#cite_note-IT20120919-50)[[51]](#cite_note-intoday1-51) Hazare rejected the poll, saying "elections require huge funds, which will be tough for activists to organise without compromising on their values". He also said it would be difficult to ensure that candidates are not corrupted once elected.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) Hazare and Kejriwal agreed on 19 September 2012 that their differences regarding a role in politics were irreconcilable. Kejriwal had support from some anti-corruption movement activists, such as [Prashant Bhushan](/wiki/Prashant_Bhushan "Prashant Bhushan") and [Shanti Bhushan](/wiki/Shanti_Bhushan "Shanti Bhushan"), but was opposed by others such as [Kiran Bedi](/wiki/Kiran_Bedi "Kiran Bedi") and [Santosh Hegde](/wiki/Santosh_Hegde "Santosh Hegde"). On 2 October,[[54]](#cite_note-54) Kejriwal announced that he was forming a political party and that he intended the formal launch to be on 26 November, coinciding with the anniversary of India's adoption of its Constitution in 1949.[[50]](#cite_note-IT20120919-50)[[51]](#cite_note-intoday1-51)

The party's name reflects the phrase *[Aam Aadmi](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi "Aam Aadmi")* (transl. common man), whose interests Kejriwal proposed to represent. A party constitution was adopted on 24 November 2012, when a National Council comprising 320 people and a National Executive of 23 were also formed. Both the Council and the Executive were expected to have more members in due course, with the intention being that all districts and all classes of people would have a voice.[[51]](#cite_note-intoday1-51) Various committees were proposed to be formed to draft proposals for adoption by the party in a process that was expected to take several months. Although one aim was to limit nepotism, there were complaints at this initial meeting that the selection of people invited to attend was itself an example of such practices.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The party was formally launched in Delhi on 26 November[[56]](#cite_note-56) and in March 2013, it was registered as a political party by the [Election Commission of India](/wiki/Election_Commission_of_India "Election Commission of India").[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[a]](#cite_note-59)

### Funding

On 26 November 2012, the formal launch day of the AAP, former law minister [Shanti Bhushan](/wiki/Shanti_Bhushan "Shanti Bhushan") donated ₹10 million (US$120,000) to the party. Prashant Bhushan, his son, was a member of the party's National Executive Committee.[[59]](#cite_note-donations-60) The party raised ₹20 crore (US$2.4 million) by November 2013 and received ₹18 crore (US$2.1 million) in 2015 assembly polls.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### Initial period

On 18 May 2013, a group of [Indian Americans](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans") from 20 different cities in the US held a convention in Chicago and extended support to the AAP. The convention was attended by two AAP leaders, [Kumar Vishwas](/wiki/Kumar_Vishwas "Kumar Vishwas") and [Yogendra Yadav](/wiki/Yogendra_Yadav "Yogendra Yadav"), and Kejriwal addressed it via video conferencing.[[61]](#cite_note-62) [Aruna Roy](/wiki/Aruna_Roy "Aruna Roy") and [Medha Patkar](/wiki/Medha_Patkar "Medha Patkar"), who had differences with Kejriwal on certain issues, supported him after his 15-day fast against inflated electricity bills.[[62]](#cite_note-end-63) On 22 March 2014, the [Janata Dal (Secular)](/wiki/Janata_Dal_(Secular) "Janata Dal (Secular)") party of Delhi announced it would merge with the Aam Aadmi Party, citing Kejriwal's tenure as Chief Minister of Delhi.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65) Later two of the founders of the party, Prashant Bhushan and Yogendra Yadav, left the Aam Aadmi Party in 2017 and formed [Swaraj Abhiyan](/wiki/Swaraj_Abhiyan "Swaraj Abhiyan").[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### Protests

On 23 March 2013, Kejriwal began an indefinite fast in an attempt to mobilise people against inflated power and electricity bills at a house in Sundar Nagri, a low-income group resettlement colony in North-East Delhi.[[66]](#cite_note-67) During the protest, he urged Delhi citizens not to pay "inflated" water and electricity bills.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The AAP also demanded an audit of power and electricity supply in Delhi by the [Comptroller and Auditor General of India](/wiki/Comptroller_and_Auditor_General_of_India "Comptroller and Auditor General of India"), something that was also supported by civil society groups like the National Alliance of People's Movement (NAPM).[[68]](#cite_note-69) The AAP claimed that the protest gathered support from 100,000 people in Delhi on a single day and more than 300,000 people up to 28 March 2013.[[69]](#cite_note-support-70) Anna Hazare urged Kejriwal to end the fast on 29 March and he did so on 6 April.[[62]](#cite_note-end-63)

On 10 June 2013, Kejriwal supported agitation by Delhi auto rickshaw drivers, who were protesting the [Delhi government's](/wiki/Government_of_Delhi "Government of Delhi") ban on advertisements on auto rickshaws. Kejriwal claimed the government's ban was because the drivers supported his party and carried AAP's advertisements on their vehicles. He said that the AAP would put 10,000 advertisements on auto rickshaws as a protest.[[70]](#cite_note-71) In retrospect, after Kejriwal had been elected and then resigned his position, a union representing the drivers expressed dissatisfaction, saying: "Arvind Kejriwal, who had won the elections because of the support of the auto drivers, has betrayed them by not fulfilling any of the promises made before the elections".[[71]](#cite_note-72) On 22 April 2015, the AAP organised a rally in Delhi against a land acquisition bill.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Ideology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Anaa_Hazare_with_Anupam_Kher%2C_Manish_Sisodia_and_Kumar_Vishwas.jpg/250px-Anaa_Hazare_with_Anupam_Kher%2C_Manish_Sisodia_and_Kumar_Vishwas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anaa_Hazare_with_Anupam_Kher,_Manish_Sisodia_and_Kumar_Vishwas.jpg)

Anna Hazare, Anupam Kher, Manish Sisodia and Kumar Vishwas during the [Jan Lokpal Bill](/wiki/Jan_Lokpal_Bill "Jan Lokpal Bill") movement

Though the party doesn't follow any specific ideology, at the time of its formation, the AAP said that the promise of equality and justice that forms a part of the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") and of its preamble has not been fulfilled and that the [Independence of India](/wiki/Independence_of_India "Independence of India") has replaced enslavement to an oppressive foreign power with that to a political elite. It claimed that the common people of India remain unheard and unseen except when it suits politicians. The AAP's goal is to reverse the way that government accountability operates, and the party takes an interpretation of the [Gandhian socialist](/wiki/Gandhian_socialism "Gandhian socialism") concept of [swaraj](/wiki/Swaraj "Swaraj") as a tenet. It believes that through *swaraj*, the government will be directly accountable to the people instead of higher officials. The *swaraj* model lays stress on self-governance, community building, and decentralization.[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

Kejriwal has stated that the AAP refuses to be guided by ideologies and that they are entering politics to change the system, saying, "We are common men. If we find our solution in the left, we are happy to borrow it from there. If we find our solution in the right, we are happy to borrow it from there."[[75]](#cite_note-76) Kejriwal has put forward 3 pillars of Aam Aadmi Party's core ideology: *staunch patriotism*, *staunch honesty* and *humanity.*[[76]](#cite_note-77)

The party advocates scrapping [Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code](/wiki/Section_377_of_the_Indian_Penal_Code "Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code") and legalizing both homosexuality and [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[77]](#cite_note-78) The party is also regarded as being [populist](/wiki/Populism "Populism")[[16]](#cite_note-London_School_of_Economics-16) and [centrist](/wiki/Centrist "Centrist").[[78]](#cite_note-ThePrint03Feb20-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

## Organisation

The party leadership consists of the following bodies:

* **National Convener**: Arvind Kejriwal
* **National Secretary**: Pankaj Gupta[[80]](#cite_note-AAP_PAC-81)
* **National Treasurer**: Narain Dass Gupta[[80]](#cite_note-AAP_PAC-81)

### Political Affairs Committee

Political Affairs Committee is the highest decision-making body of the party.[[81]](#cite_note-Newsmaker-82) The current member of the PAC are [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal"), [Manish Sisodia](/wiki/Manish_Sisodia "Manish Sisodia"), [Sanjay Singh](/wiki/Sanjay_Singh_(Uttar_Pradesh_politician) "Sanjay Singh (Uttar Pradesh politician)"), [Gopal Rai](/wiki/Gopal_Rai "Gopal Rai"), [Atishi](/wiki/Atishi "Atishi"), [Imran Hussain](/wiki/Imran_Hussain_(Indian_politician) "Imran Hussain (Indian politician)"), [Durgesh Pathak](/wiki/Durgesh_Pathak "Durgesh Pathak"), [Raghav Chadha](/wiki/Raghav_Chadha "Raghav Chadha") and [Rakhi Bidlan](/wiki/Rakhi_Bidlan "Rakhi Bidlan"). Pankaj Kr Gupta (National Secretary) and Narain Dass Gupta (National Treasurer) are the ex officio members of the PAC.[[80]](#cite_note-AAP_PAC-81)

### National Executive Committee

The party's National Executive Committee had 34 members in 2022.[[82]](#cite_note-AAP_NEC-83)

### State Leadership

The AAP has state wings in Andhra Pradesh, Assam, Bihar, Chandigarh, Chhattisgarh, [Delhi](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi_Party,_Delhi "Aam Aadmi Party, Delhi"), Goa, Gujarat, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, Karnataka, Kerala, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Manipur, Mizoram,[[83]](#cite_note-84) [Punjab](/wiki/Aam_Aadmi_Party,_Punjab "Aam Aadmi Party, Punjab"), Puducherry, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand and West Bengal.

### 2013 Delhi Assembly election

Main article: [2013 Delhi Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2013_Delhi_Legislative_Assembly_election "2013 Delhi Legislative Assembly election")

The 2013 Delhi state assembly elections were the party's first electoral contest. The Election Commission approved the symbol of a broom for use by the AAP in that campaign.[[91]](#cite_note-92) The party said that its candidates were honest and had been screened for potential criminal backgrounds.[[92]](#cite_note-93) It published its central manifesto on 20 November 2013, promising to implement the Jan Lokpal Bill within 15 days of coming to power.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

In November 2013, a sting operation conducted by Media Sarkar alleged that several leaders of the AAP, including Kumar Vishwas and [Shazia Ilmi](/wiki/Shazia_Ilmi "Shazia Ilmi"), had agreed to extend their support to some people seeking assistance with land deals and other financial arrangements in return for donations in cash to the AAP.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Ilmi offered to withdraw her candidature as a result, but the party refused to accept her offer, describing the footage as fabricated and a violation of the Model Code of Conduct.[[95]](#cite_note-96) The Election Commission ordered an inquiry regarding the legitimacy of the video where the political leaders have raised funding through "illegal" means.[[96]](#cite_note-97) However immediately after the general elections, Shazia Ilmi (PAC member) resigned from the party.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

The AAP emerged as the second-largest party in Delhi, winning 28 of the 70 Assembly seats; the Bharatiya Janata Party, as the largest party, won 31, while its ally [Shiromani Akali Dal](/wiki/Shiromani_Akali_Dal "Shiromani Akali Dal"), won 1; Indian National Congress won 8, and two were won by others.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) On 28 December 2013, the AAP formed a minority government in the hung Assembly, with what Sheila Dikshit describes as "not unconditional" support from Indian National Congress.[[100]](#cite_note-101) Kejriwal became the second-youngest [Chief Minister of Delhi](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_Delhi "Chief Minister of Delhi").[[101]](#cite_note-102) As a result of the Delhi elections, the AAP became a recognised state party in Delhi.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

### 2014 general election

Main article: [2014 Indian general election](/wiki/2014_Indian_general_election "2014 Indian general election")

The AAP fielded 434 candidates in the [2014 Indian general election](/wiki/2014_Indian_general_election "2014 Indian general election"), in which it did not expect to do well. It recognised that its support was based primarily in urban areas and that different strategies might be required for different regions of the country. The party pointed out that its funding was limited and that there were too many demands for local visits from Kejriwal. The intention was to field candidates in large numbers to maximise the likelihood of recognition as a national party by the Election Commission.[[103]](#cite_note-IndExp20140105-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) The outcome was that four AAP candidates won, all from [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_(India) "Punjab (India)").[[105]](#cite_note-106)

Consequently, the AAP became a recognised state party in Punjab.[[106]](#cite_note-107) The party obtained 2% of all votes cast nationwide and 414 of its candidates forfeited their [deposit](/wiki/Deposit_(politics) "Deposit (politics)") by failing to secure one-sixth of the vote in their constituencies.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Although the party secured 32.9 per cent of the votes in Delhi, it failed to win any seats there.[[108]](#cite_note-109) AAP convenor, Arvind Kejriwal fought from [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi_Lok_Sabha_constituency "Varanasi Lok Sabha constituency") against BJP's Prime Ministerial candidate [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi"), but lost by a margin of 371,784 (20.30%) votes and came second ahead of BSP, Congress, SP.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

The National Executive member Yogendra Yadav in a letter to his party members criticised Kejriwal's style of leadership.[[110]](#cite_note-toi20140606-111) After the National Executive meeting on 8 June, the party and Kejriwal acknowledged these differences and announced the launch of "Mission Vistar" (Mission Expand), to include more people in local as well as national decision making.[[111]](#cite_note-112)

### 2015 Delhi Assembly election

The Delhi state assembly elections for the [Sixth Legislative Assembly of Delhi](/wiki/Sixth_Legislative_Assembly_of_Delhi "Sixth Legislative Assembly of Delhi") were held on 7 February 2015, as declared by the Election Commission of India.[[112]](#cite_note-113) The Aam Aadmi Party scored a landslide victory by winning a majority of 67 of the 70 seats. The BJP was able to win 3 seats and the Congress party saw all its candidates lose.[[113]](#cite_note-114) Kejriwal became the Chief Minister for the second time.[[114]](#cite_note-115) The AAP had started campaigning in Delhi in November 2014 and declared candidates for all 70 seats.[[115]](#cite_note-116)

During the campaign, Kejriwal claimed that the BJP had been trying to bribe AAP volunteers. He asked Delhi voters to not deny the bribes offered to them. He suggested that voters should accept the bribe from others and yet vote for AAP through the secret ballot in the election.[[116]](#cite_note-117) The situation caused the Election Commission of India to instruct Kejriwal to desist from breaking laws governing the model code of conduct for elections in India,[[117]](#cite_note-118) but the Delhi court then allowed Kejriwal to challenge this.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

The President's Rule was subsequently rescinded and Kejriwal became the Chief Minister of Delhi with six cabinet ministers ([Manish Sisodia](/wiki/Manish_Sisodia "Manish Sisodia"), [Asim Ahmed Khan](/wiki/Asim_Ahmed_Khan "Asim Ahmed Khan"), [Sandeep Kumar](/wiki/Sandeep_Kumar_(politician) "Sandeep Kumar (politician)"), [Satyendar Jain](/wiki/Satyendar_Jain "Satyendar Jain"), [Gopal Rai](/wiki/Gopal_Rai "Gopal Rai"), and [Jitender Singh Tomar](/wiki/Jitender_Singh_Tomar "Jitender Singh Tomar")).[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Major differences surfaced within the party leadership soon after its victory. It created deep fissures between the founding members who had together championed the anti-corruption movement. Problems emerged in February 2015 when Yogendra Yadav and Prashanth Bhushan wrote a joint letter to the National Executive, highlighting Kejriwal's tendency to unilateral decision-making, which they alleged had compromised the party's core principle of Swaraj.[[121]](#cite_note-122) After continued allegations, counter-allegations and several failed attempts at reconciliation between the two sides, Yadav and Bhushan were first removed from the PAC and later from the National Executive after the party's National Council passed a resolution to expel them for their alleged anti-party activities.[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125) Party leaders refuted accusations made by Yadav and Bhushan at the meeting that the party was murdering democracy and resorting to intimidation.[[125]](#cite_note-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127) In April 2015, Yadav, Bhushan, Anand Kumar, and Ajit Jha were removed from the party.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

### 2017 State Assembly elections

For the first time, the AAP contested the [2017 Goa Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2017_Goa_Legislative_Assembly_election "2017 Goa Legislative Assembly election") and [2017 Punjab Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2017_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2017 Punjab Legislative Assembly election"). In Goa the AAP could not win any seat, and 38 out of 39 candidates failed to save their security deposits.[[128]](#cite_note-129) For the [2017 Punjab Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2017_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2017 Punjab Legislative Assembly election"), the [Lok Insaaf Party](/wiki/Lok_Insaaf_Party "Lok Insaaf Party") allied with the AAP. This alliance was called the *AAP Alliance* and was represented on news channels as *AAP+*. It won 22 seats in total, two of which were won by the Lok Insaaf Party and the other twenty by the AAP.[[129]](#cite_note-130)

### 2019 general election

Main article: [2019 Indian general election](/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election "2019 Indian general election")

Unlike the [2014 Indian general election](/wiki/2014_Indian_general_election "2014 Indian general election"), the Political Affairs Committee (PAC) of the party decided to contest elections on limited seats of some of the states and all the seats in [Delhi](/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election_in_Delhi "2019 Indian general election in Delhi"), [Goa](/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election_in_Goa "2019 Indian general election in Goa"),[[130]](#cite_note-131) and [Punjab](/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election_in_Punjab "2019 Indian general election in Punjab").[[131]](#cite_note-132) In the state of [Haryana](/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"), the AAP formed an alliance with [Dushyant Chautala](/wiki/Dushyant_Chautala "Dushyant Chautala")'s [Jannayak Janata Party](/wiki/Jannayak_Janata_Party "Jannayak Janata Party") to contest three [Lok Sabha constituencies](/wiki/List_of_constituencies_of_the_Lok_Sabha "List of constituencies of the Lok Sabha").[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134) The PAC also decided to support and campaign for [CPI (M)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_India_(Marxist) "Communist Party of India (Marxist)") in [Kerala](/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election_in_Kerala "2019 Indian general election in Kerala").[[134]](#cite_note-135) The party also fielded its first [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") candidate from [Allahabad](/wiki/Allahabad_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Allahabad (Lok Sabha constituency)") in [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh").[[135]](#cite_note-Trans-136) The AAP won only 1 constituency of [Sangrur](/wiki/Sangrur_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Sangrur (Lok Sabha constituency)").

Voting for the Delhi Assembly elections took place on 8 February 2020, following vehement campaigns run by the major political parties contesting the election.[[136]](#cite_note-D0-137) The counting of votes and subsequent announcement of results happened on 11 February.[[136]](#cite_note-D0-137)

The Aam Aadmi Party retained the government as the party won 62 out of 70 seats.[[35]](#cite_note-Delhi_2020_Hindu-35)[[137]](#cite_note-138) [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal") became the [Chief Minister of Delhi](/wiki/List_of_chief_ministers_of_Delhi "List of chief ministers of Delhi") for the third consecutive time. The party's vote share was 53.5%, according to the results.[[138]](#cite_note-139)

### 2021 Chandigarh Municipal Corporation election

The AAP contested in [2021 Chandigarh Municipal Corporation election](/wiki/2021_Chandigarh_Municipal_Corporation_election "2021 Chandigarh Municipal Corporation election") for the first time, won 14 seats and became the single largest party in the council of total 35 elected seats.[[139]](#cite_note-NDTV_27_Dec-140) Sitting mayor Ravi Kant Sharma from BJP lost his seat to AAP candidate Damanpreet Singh.[[140]](#cite_note-Tribuneindia_Full_list_MC_2021-141)[[141]](#cite_note-outlookindia_27_Dec_21-142) In ward number 21, former mayor and BJP candidate Davesh Moudgil was defeated by AAP's Jasbir.[[141]](#cite_note-outlookindia_27_Dec_21-142)

### 2022 Punjab Assembly election

In January 2021, Arvind Kejriwal announced that AAP would be contesting [six state elections in 2022](/wiki/2022_elections_in_India "2022 elections in India"). The six states were [Uttar Pradesh](#Uttar_Pradesh), [Himachal Pradesh](#Himachal_Pradesh), [Goa](#Goa), [Gujarat](#Gujarat), [Uttarakhand](#Uttarakhand), and [Punjab](#Punjab).[[142]](#cite_note-6_States_HT_28_Jan_2021-143) The party won a landslide victory in [Punjab](/wiki/2022_Punjab_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Punjab Legislative Assembly election"), defeating the incumbent Congress government of [Charanjit Singh Channi](/wiki/Charanjit_Singh_Channi "Charanjit Singh Channi"), and state party convener [Bhagwant Mann](/wiki/Bhagwant_Mann "Bhagwant Mann") was sworn in as the new CM. The party also gained two seats in [Goa](/wiki/2022_Goa_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Goa Legislative Assembly election") and five seats in [Gujarat](/wiki/2022_Gujarat_Legislative_Assembly_election "2022 Gujarat Legislative Assembly election").

### 2025 Delhi Legislative Assembly election

Vote share by party in 2025 Delhi Legislative assembly election

1. National Democratic Alliance (47.1%)
2. Aam Aadmi Party (43.6%)
3. Indian National Congress (6.34%)
4. Others (2.37%)
5. NOTA (0.57%)

The incumbent Aam Aadmi Party, which was in power for the previous ten years, lost the election, with several prominent leaders and cabinet ministers including national convener [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal"), [Manish Sisodia](/wiki/Manish_Sisodia "Manish Sisodia"), [Satyendra Kumar Jain](/wiki/Satyendra_Kumar_Jain "Satyendra Kumar Jain"), [Somnath Bharti](/wiki/Somnath_Bharti "Somnath Bharti"), [Saurabh Bhardwaj](/wiki/Saurabh_Bhardwaj "Saurabh Bhardwaj"), [Rakhi Birla](/wiki/Rakhi_Birla "Rakhi Birla"), and [Durgesh Pathak](/wiki/Durgesh_Pathak "Durgesh Pathak") losing their seats. AAP managed to win only 22 seats, which was the party's worst performance in Delhi to date. Its vote share plummeted to 43.57%. The BJP formed the government with 48 seats.
